text: Engineered surface strain is a commonly used variable to modify the surface chemistry and catalytic performances of nanoparticles (NPs). Here, we show that the surface strain of the outer platinum (Pt) shell of a core/shell FePt/Pt NP, which consists of a 3 Pt layer thick shell on top of a fcc solid solution core of an intermetallic fct FePt alloy, can be systematically adjusted by transitioning the FePt alloy core from the fcc state to the fct state. It is demonstrated that fct-FePt/Pt NPs have greater catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The improved catalytic performance of the fct-FePt/Pt NPs was elucidated using the hybrid quantum mechanics–molecular mechanics (QM–MM) method, which indicated that the fct core alleviated the overcompressed state of the Pt shell. In addition, by partially replacing Fe with Cu to form fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs, the catalytic performances of the NPs were further enhanced, exhibiting the highest specific ORR activity that is approximately 10 times higher than that of a luxury grade Pt catalyst. This work thus provides a new way to systematically regulate the surface strain of NPs to improve their catalytic activities.
explanation: The text is highly technical and coherent, consistent with human writing; however, an odd phrase ('luxury grade Pt catalyst') and some phrasing suggest possible AI-generated origin.
